Journal.Title: Burns : journal of the International Society for Burn Injuries

Article.ArticleTitle: Cold atmospheric plasma in burn treatment: A review of experimental evidence and future clinical translatability.

Abstract.AbstractText: Burn wounds are complex injuries with many barriers to healing, with high inflammation, dysregulated tissue repair, and high susceptibility to infection. Current treatments may fail to overcome these challenges, and patients frequently experience poor outcomes and severe scarring. A new treatment, cold atmospheric plasma, is being developed to combine both infection control and healing stimulation without the use of antibiotics, which are themselves being used sparingly to reduce the emergence of resistance. Plasma is a multifaceted agent and contributes a range of reactive species to interact with many critical pathways in the healing process. This review will discuss the aspects of healing and infection which are influenced by plasma, and will assess the current knowledge base in terms of translatability to eventual clinical use for burn management.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declaration of Competing Interest The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Ruth Simpson reports financial support was provided by Commonwealth of Australia in the form of a Research Training Program scholarship (DOI: https://doi.org/10.82133/C42F-K220). If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
